era_context: The Western Roman Empire is in terminal decline. The Visigothic kingdom under Alaric I has been operating within and against Roman territory for over a decade. The Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire under Theodosius II remains administratively intact but politically fractious. In Britain, Roman military withdrawal is complete or nearly so; in North Africa, Vandal pressure is mounting against Roman provinces. This is the era of the 'barbarian invasions'—better understood as migration, settlement, and the fragmentation of imperial authority.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Rhine and Danube (sparse Roman documentation)
East Asia (contemporary Chinese sources exist but may be underrepresented in Western training data)

events.title: Visigothic sack of Rome under Alaric I
events.description: Alaric I, king of the Visigoths, led his forces into Rome on 24 August 410 CE after a three-year siege and negotiation period. The city was systematically plundered over three days; temples, public buildings, and private residences were ransacked. The Visigothic forces did not massacre the population indiscriminately, and churches were largely spared (Alaric was Arian Christian). This was the first sack of Rome by a foreign power in nearly 800 years and sent shockwaves through the Mediterranean world. Alaric died shortly after, and his successor Athaulf led the Visigoths toward Gaul and eventually Hispania, where they would establish a kingdom.
key_figures: Stilicho (deceased, but his legacy shaped events)

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans (Historiae adversum paganos)
Augustine of Hippo, City of God (De civitate Dei), Books 1–3
Ammianus Marcellinus, Roman History (fragmentary for this period)

events.certainty_note: The date (24 August 410) is attested in multiple near-contemporary sources. Augustine was writing within a generation. The event's occurrence is not disputed, though interpretations of its significance vary.

events.title: Roman military withdrawal from Britain
events.description: In 410 CE, the Roman military presence in Britain effectively ended. The exact sequence is debated: either Emperor Honorius formally withdrew troops to defend Italy against Alaric, or the withdrawal was already underway and formalized in 410. The 'Rescript of Honorius' (attested only in Zosimus) instructed Britons to defend themselves. Roman legions departed, leaving Britain without imperial military protection for the first time in nearly 400 years. This opened the way for Anglo-Saxon settlement and marked the beginning of the end of Roman Britain as a cultural and administrative entity.
key_figures: Zosimus (chronicler, only source for the rescript)

sources.name: Bede, Ecclesiastical History of the English People (Historia ecclesiastica gentis Anglorum)
Archaeological evidence: cessation of Roman coin deposition, abandonment of military sites

events.certainty_note: The withdrawal is archaeologically confirmed (coin and pottery evidence), but the exact date and mechanism are debated. Zosimus is the only source for the 'Rescript of Honorius,' and his account is written a century later. Some scholars argue the withdrawal was gradual (408–410 or later); others place it firmly in 410.
cross_references: Part of broader Roman military retrenchment in 408–410
Led to Anglo-Saxon settlement and the end of Roman Britain

events.title: Athaulf becomes king of the Visigoths
events.description: Following Alaric I's death (probably in late 410 or early 411), his brother-in-law Athaulf assumed leadership of the Visigothic people. Athaulf was more pragmatic than Alaric and sought accommodation with the Roman Empire rather than continued warfare. He married Galla Placidia, the half-sister of Emperor Honorius, in 414, cementing a political alliance. Under Athaulf, the Visigoths began their migration toward Gaul and Hispania, eventually establishing a kingdom that would last until the Umayyad conquest in 711.

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Jordanes, Getica (The Origin and Deeds of the Goths)

events.certainty_note: Athaulf's succession is attested in multiple sources, but the exact date of Alaric's death is uncertain (late 410 or early 411). The marriage to Galla Placidia is dated to 414 in most sources.

events.title: Usurper Constantine III controls Gaul and Hispania
events.description: Constantine III, a Roman general proclaimed emperor by his troops in Britain in 407, had by 410 established control over much of Gaul and Hispania. He ruled from Arles and maintained the facade of legitimate imperial authority, minting coins and issuing edicts. However, his authority was contested by the legitimate Western Roman Emperor Honorius and was increasingly challenged by Visigothic and other barbarian movements. Constantine III's regime represented the fragmentation of imperial authority in the western provinces and would eventually collapse by 411.
key_figures: Gerontius (Roman general, rival to Constantine III)

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Numismatic evidence: coins minted in Constantine III's name

events.certainty_note: Constantine III's rule in Gaul and Hispania in 410 is well attested in numismatic and literary sources. His eventual defeat in 411 is also documented.
cross_references: Follows from 407-001 (Constantine III's proclamation in Britain)

disconfirming_evidence: The 'sack of Rome' narrative has been complicated by modern scholarship. While the plundering is confirmed, the scale and nature of destruction were likely less catastrophic than later Christian and pagan sources suggested. Augustine and Orosius, writing to defend Christianity against pagan accusations that Rome fell due to abandoning the old gods, may have exaggerated the violence. Archaeological evidence shows that Rome remained a functioning city after 410, with continued occupation and repair of buildings. The event was symbolically catastrophic but materially less devastating than the rhetoric suggests. Additionally, the 'barbarian invasion' framing is now widely rejected by scholars in favor of 'migration period' or 'late antique transformation,' emphasizing continuity and integration rather than conquest and destruction.
historiographic_note: The year 410 is exceptionally well documented for the sack of Rome, with multiple near-contemporary sources (Augustine, Orosius) and later chronicles (Zosimus, Jordanes). However, sources are heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Italy; events in Britain, Gaul, and the Rhine frontier are less thoroughly documented. The sources are also predominantly Christian (Augustine, Orosius) or written from a Roman perspective, potentially biasing the narrative toward catastrophism. Archaeological evidence (coins, pottery, building remains) provides a corrective to literary sources but is unevenly distributed geographically. Events in Asia, Africa, and northern Europe are largely absent from the surviving record.

graph_edges.note: The sack of Rome was the culmination of Alaric's three-year siege and negotiation, which began in 408.
The sack of Rome and Alaric's death led to Visigothic settlement in Aquitaine under Athaulf.
The Roman withdrawal from Britain opened the way for Anglo-Saxon settlement, which accelerated after 449.
Athaulf's succession led to his marriage to Galla Placidia in 414, cementing Visigothic-Roman alliance.
Constantine III's rule in Gaul and Hispania was challenged and eventually defeated in 411.
